1433	[Associator for $\otimes_X$ (unconditional)]
1434	  \label{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}
1435	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_assoc_iso}
1436	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj,
1437	        lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}
1438	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
1439	  \(M, N, P \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). There is an isomorphism
1440	  \[
1441	    (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
1442	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1443	    M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)
1444	  \]
1445	  in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), natural in \(M, N, P\). The group law on
1446	  \(\otimes\)-iso-classes (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) consumes only
1447	  the \emph{existence} of this isomorphism, the proposition
1448	  \(\mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \cong M \otimes_X (N
1449	  \otimes_X P)\bigr)\); no pentagon, triangle, or naturality coherence of it is
1450	  ever required.
1451	
1452	  The Lean pin is unconditional: it holds for arbitrary
1453	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(M, N, P\), with no local-triviality or
1454	  invertibility hypothesis. (Earlier drafts carried vestigial
1455	  \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\) hypotheses to match the carrier; these were
1456	  dropped once the construction below was seen to use none of them.)
1457	\end{lemma}
1458	
1459	\begin{proof}
1460	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj,
1461	        lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}
1462	  \textbf{Realization (sheafification transport, unconditional via d.2).} The
1463	  associator is the sheafification transport of the presheaf-of-modules associator
1464	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategoryStruct.associator}\), which exists for
1465	  \emph{all} presheaves of modules over the varying ring. The transport requires the
1466	  single left-whisker \(F \triangleleft \mathtt{toSheafify}\) to lie in the
1467	  sheafification localizer \(J.W\) for the relevant \(F\); that single open
1468	  left-whiskering obligation \(\mathtt{isLocallyInjective\_whiskerLeft\_of\_W}\) is
1469	  closed \emph{unconditionally} --- for an arbitrary whiskering object, with no
1470	  flatness and no local triviality --- by the d.2 stalk--tensor commutation
1471	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk} (\cref{sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor}):
1472	  a \(J.W\)-morphism \(g\) is a stalkwise isomorphism, and by the d.2 commutation
1473	  \(\bigl(F \triangleleft g\bigr)_x \cong \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes g_x\) is again an
1474	  isomorphism, so \(F \triangleleft g \in J.W\). Hence the associator is itself
1475	  unconditional and axiom-clean once d.2 (\cref{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}) is
1476	  formalized; it does \emph{not} require the full \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance
1477	  or the \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) monoidal category --- only the one left-whisker
1478	  lemma --- and no sectionwise flatness enters. The local-gluing description below
1479	  records the equivalent concrete local realization through the
1480	  restriction-compatibility comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}.
1481	
1482	  Concretely, the same isomorphism is described by gluing canonical local isomorphisms
1483	  through the restriction-compatibility comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}.
1484	  Fix a
1485	  common open cover \(\{U\}\) of \(X\). On each member \(U\) form the canonical
1486	  composite
1487	  \[
1488	    \bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P\bigr)\big|_U
1489	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1490	    \bigl(M|_U \otimes_U N|_U\bigr) \otimes_U P|_U
1491	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1492	    M|_U \otimes_U \bigl(N|_U \otimes_U P|_U\bigr)
1493	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1494	    \bigl(M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\bigr)\big|_U ,
1495	  \]
1496	  in which:
1497	  \begin{itemize}
1498	    \item the first arrow is two applications of the restriction-compatibility
1499	      isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} along the open immersion
1500	      \(U \hookrightarrow X\): first \((M \otimes_X N)|_U \cong M|_U \otimes_U
1501	      N|_U\) tensored with \(P|_U\), then \(\bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X
1502	      P\bigr)|_U \cong (M \otimes_X N)|_U \otimes_U P|_U\);
1503	    \item the middle arrow is the \emph{canonical presheaf-level associator}
1504	      \(\alpha\) of the (symmetric) monoidal category
1505	      \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_U\)
1506	      (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategoryStruct.associator}\),
1507	      \cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}(i)), read on the restricted modules;
1508	    \item the third arrow is again two applications of
1509	      \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, in the opposite direction.
1510	  \end{itemize}
1511	  Each of the three arrows is a \emph{canonical} isomorphism: the
1512	  restriction-compatibility comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is induced
1513	  by the open immersion with no choices, hence natural in its module arguments, and
1514	  the presheaf associator \(\alpha\) is a fixed natural transformation. Therefore,
1515	  on a double overlap \(U \cap U'\), restricting the \(U\)-composite and the
1516	  \(U'\)-composite to \(U \cap U'\) yields the same morphism: each of the three
1517	  arrows commutes with the further restriction to \(U \cap U'\) by the naturality of
1518	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} and of the presheaf associator, so the two local
1519	  composites agree on the overlap.
1520	
1521	  Because the internal hom \(\mathcal{H}om(-,-)\) of sheaves of
1522	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules is itself a sheaf on \(X\), a family of morphisms defined
1523	  on the members of an open cover and agreeing on the overlaps glues to a single
1524	  global morphism. Applying this both to the local composites above and to their
1525	  local inverses produces a global morphism
1526	  \((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \to M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\) whose restriction
1527	  to each \(U\) is the local composite, together with a two-sided inverse glued from
1528	  the local inverses; hence the glued morphism is a global isomorphism --- the
1529	  associator. The single non-formal input of this re-route is the
1530	  restriction-compatibility comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}; the presheaf
1531	  associator (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}(i)) and the sheaf-of-modules
1532	  internal-hom gluing are already available.
1533	
1534	  Note that, unlike the pointwise-existence statement
1535	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial} --- a \emph{proposition} proved
1536	  cover-pointwise with \emph{no} overlap obligation --- the associator is
1537	  \emph{global data}, a single isomorphism of sheaves on all of \(X\). Assembling it
1538	  from local pieces is therefore genuinely stronger than that pattern: the
1539	  overlap-naturality compatibility of the local composites must be discharged before
1540	  gluing, and is discharged above. The locally-trivial hypotheses of the statement
1541	  are not consumed; the comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is applied to the
1542	  global, untrivialised restrictions \((M \otimes_X N)|_U\), so no free-cover
1543	  specialisation can replace it. What this proof does \emph{not} require is the
1544	  \emph{full} \((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance or the
1545	  \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) monoidal category
1546	  (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W,lem:jw_ismonoidal}): only the single left-whisker obligation
1547	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}, closed via the d.2 stalk--tensor
1548	  commutation \cref{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}, is consumed by the sheafification
1549	  transport. The group law on \(\otimes\)-iso-classes
1550	  consumes only the \emph{existence} of the resulting isomorphism, never its pentagon,
1551	  triangle, or naturality coherence.
1552	\end{proof}
1553

1607	\begin{lemma}
1608	
1609	
1610	\leanok
1611	[Inverse of an invertible module]
1612	  \label{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}
1613	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.exists_tensorObj_inverse}
1614	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial,
1615	        lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
1616	  % NOTE: ON the critical path under the PRIMARY locally-trivial group law (it
1617	  % supplies inverses to lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup, in whose uses it sits).
1618	  % Only under the route-(e) fallback is it dispensable: there the group law uses
1619	  % the tensor-invertibility predicate IsInvertible (def:scheme_modules_isinvertible),
1620	  % which carries the inverse existentially, so no separate ``a tensor inverse
1621	  % exists'' lemma is required.
1622	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CR (Modules on Ringed Spaces, \S Invertible
1623	  % modules), Lemma~lemma-constructions-invertible (item~2: the dual of an
1624	  % invertible is invertible and the contraction map is an isomorphism) +
1625	  % Definition~definition-pic (the Picard group as the abelian group of
1626	  % iso-classes of invertibles)
1627	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4200--L4213 and L4350--L4357).
1628	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1629	  % "\begin{lemma}
1630	  % \label{lemma-constructions-invertible}
1631	  % Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
1632	  % ...
1633	  % \item If $\mathcal{L}$ is an invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-module, then so is
1634	  % $\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X)$ and the evaluation map
1635	  % $\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
1636	  % \SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X) \to \mathcal{O}_X$
1637	  % is an isomorphism.
1638	  % ...
1639	  % \begin{definition}
1640	  % \label{definition-pic}
1641	  % Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
1642	  % The {\it Picard group} $\Pic(X)$ of $X$ is the
1643	  % abelian group whose elements are isomorphism classes of
1644	  % invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules, with addition
1645	  % corresponding to tensor product.
1646	  % \end{definition}"
1647	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag~01CR, Lemma~\texttt{lemma-constructions-%
1648	  invertible} (item~2, the dual of an invertible is invertible and the contraction
1649	  is an isomorphism) and Definition~\texttt{definition-pic} (the Picard group is
1650	  the abelian group of iso-classes of invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules under
1651	  tensor product).}
1652	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(L \in \texttt{LineBundle}\,X\) be a line
1653	  bundle. The dual
1654	  \[
1655	    L^{-1} \;\;:=\;\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)
1656	  \]
1657	  is again a line bundle on \(X\), and there is a canonical natural
1658	  isomorphism
1659	  \(\varepsilon_L : L \otimes_X L^{-1} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_X\).
1660	  Consequently every line bundle has a two-sided tensor inverse under
1661	  \(\otimes_X\), and the monoid \(\bigl(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X / \sim, \otimes_X,
1662	  \mathcal{O}_X\bigr)\) of isomorphism classes is an abelian group ---
1663	  the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) (Stacks tag 01CR).
1664	\end{lemma}
1665	
1666	\begin{proof}
1667	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso,
1668	        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso, lem:internal_hom_eval, lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial}
1669	  \textbf{Infrastructure-blocked.} This statement is \emph{not} realizable with the
1670	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module infrastructure presently available: its construction
1671	  depends on a \emph{sheaf internal hom of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules}
1672	  \(\mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)\), an object that does not yet
1673	  exist at the presheaf, sheaf, or general-categorical level (there is no
1674	  monoidal-closed / internal-hom / dual structure on
1675	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) or \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\), and no object-level
1676	  descent assembling a global \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module from local pieces). The dual
1677	  object \(L^{-1}\) therefore cannot even be \emph{named} until that infrastructure
1678	  is built. The construction of the required internal hom, its dual, and its
1679	  evaluation is decomposed into individually-formalizable sub-steps in
1680	  \cref{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra} (\cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom} through
1681	  \cref{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial}); the present lemma is realized once those land,
1682	  and its Lean body is a placeholder until then.
1683	
1684	  We record the intended mathematical route, all three steps of which are correct
1685	  modulo the missing object. \emph{The route is as follows.} Take
1686	  \(L^{-1} := \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)\) to be the
1687	  sheaf-level dual of \cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf}, and let
1688	  \(\varepsilon_L : L \otimes_X L^{-1} \to \mathcal{O}_X\) be the evaluation of
1689	  \cref{lem:internal_hom_eval}.
1690	
1691	  \emph{Step 1 (\(L^{-1}\) is a line bundle).} Local triviality of \(L\)
1692	  (\(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,L\)) furnishes an open cover \(\{U\}\) of
1693	  \(X\) with trivialisations \(L|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\). The internal hom commutes
1694	  with restriction along the open immersion \(U \hookrightarrow X\), so on each \(U\)
1695	  \[
1696	    L^{-1}\big|_U \;\cong\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_U}(L|_U, \mathcal{O}_U)
1697	                 \;\cong\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_U}(\mathcal{O}_U, \mathcal{O}_U)
1698	                 \;\cong\; \mathcal{O}_U ,
1699	  \]
1700	  the last identification being the evaluation-at-\(1\) isomorphism for the dual of a
1701	  free rank-one module. Hence \(L^{-1}\) is locally trivial of rank one, i.e.\ an
1702	  object of \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X\).
1703	
1704	  \emph{Step 2 (the contraction is a local isomorphism).} The evaluation/contraction
1705	  morphism
1706	  \[
1707	    \varepsilon_L \;\colon\; L \otimes_X L^{-1}
1708	      \;=\; L \otimes_X \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)
1709	      \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal{O}_X,
1710	    \qquad s \otimes \phi \longmapsto \phi(s),
1711	  \]
1712	  is a morphism in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). Restricting to a trivialising
1713	  open \(U\), the restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
1714	  commutes \(\otimes_X\) past \((-)|_U\):
1715	  \[
1716	    (L \otimes_X L^{-1})\big|_U \;\cong\; L|_U \otimes_U L^{-1}|_U
1717	      \;\cong\; \mathcal{O}_U \otimes_U \mathcal{O}_U ,
1718	  \]
1719	  using the trivialisations of Step~1. Under these identifications \(\varepsilon_L|_U\)
1720	  becomes the contraction \(\mathcal{O}_U \otimes_U \mathcal{O}_U \to \mathcal{O}_U\) of
1721	  the free rank-one module with its dual, which is precisely the left unitor
1722	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso} (\(\mathcal{O}_U \otimes_U (-) \cong (-)\)) and hence an
1723	  isomorphism. Thus \(\varepsilon_L|_U\) is an isomorphism on each member of the cover.
1724	
1725	  \emph{Step 3 (glue to a global isomorphism).} The local trivialisations are induced
1726	  by the open immersions with no choices, so the local contraction isomorphisms
1727	  \(\varepsilon_L|_U\) agree on the overlaps \(U \cap U'\) by the naturality of
1728	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} and of the unitor under further restriction. As
1729	  ``being an isomorphism'' is local on \(X\) --- a morphism of sheaves of
1730	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules whose restriction to every member of an open cover is an
1731	  isomorphism is itself an isomorphism --- the globally-defined \(\varepsilon_L\) is an
1732	  isomorphism \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_X\). Equivalently, the
1733	  local inverses glue (they agree on overlaps for the same reason) to a two-sided
1734	  inverse of \(\varepsilon_L\).
1735	
1736	  Consequently, \emph{once \(L^{-1}\) and \(\varepsilon_L\) are available from
1737	  \cref{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra}}, every line bundle \(L\) has the two-sided tensor
1738	  inverse \(L^{-1}\) under \(\otimes_X\), and the monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes of
1739	  line bundles is an abelian group --- the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) (Stacks tag 01CR).
1740	  The local-triviality of \(L^{-1}\) (Step~1) is \cref{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial} and
1741	  the local-iso\(\Rightarrow\)global-iso passage (Step~3) is the already-closed
1742	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}; the single non-available input is the dual object
1743	  and its evaluation of \cref{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra}.
1744	\end{proof}

1790	\begin{lemma}
1791	[\(\pi_T^*\) is a tensor-functor]
1792	  \label{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}
1793	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.LineBundle.OnProduct.pullback_tensorObj_iso}
1794	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
1795	  % NOTE: pullback preserves the structure sheaf and tensor products (and hence
1796	  % tensor-invertibility) by the standard pullback-tensor compatibility of
1797	  % \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules (Stacks \texttt{lemma-tensor-product-pullback} /
1798	  % \texttt{lemma-pullback-invertible}), independently of the open-immersion
1799	  % restriction-compatibility iso.
1800	  With notation as in \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}, the pullback
1801	  functor
1802	  \[
1803	    \pi_T^{*} \;\;\colon\;\; \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \;\longrightarrow\;
1804	                          \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T
1805	  \]
1806	  of \cref{def:pullback_along_projection} is a (strong) monoidal
1807	  functor: there is a canonical natural isomorphism
1808	  \(\pi_T^*(\mathcal{N} \otimes_T \mathcal{N'})
1809	     \xrightarrow{\sim}
1810	   \pi_T^* \mathcal{N} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \pi_T^* \mathcal{N'}\)
1811	  for \(\mathcal{N}, \mathcal{N'} \in \texttt{LineBundle}\,T\), and a
1812	  canonical isomorphism
1813	  \(\pi_T^* \mathcal{O}_T \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T}\),
1814	  both inherited from the analogous data on the underlying
1815	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\) functors via the
1816	  pullback-tensor-compatibility of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules.
1817	\end{lemma}
1818	
1819	\begin{proof}
1820	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
1821	  On any affine open of \(T\) over which \(\mathcal{N}\) and
1822	  \(\mathcal{N'}\) trivialise simultaneously, the assertion reduces to
1823	  the standard fact that the extension of scalars
1824	  \(B \otimes_A (M \otimes_A M')
1825	   \;\cong\; (B \otimes_A M) \otimes_B (B \otimes_A M')\)
1826	  for a ring map \(A \to B\) and \(A\)-modules \(M, M'\). This affine
1827	  identity glues across an affine trivialising cover of \(T\): on each
1828	  affine open where \(\mathcal{N}\) and \(\mathcal{N}'\) are free of rank one
1829	  over \(A\), both sides evaluate to the free rank-one \(B\)-module, so
1830	  the comparison is an isomorphism. No flatness of \(A \to B\) is required
1831	  at this level, as the modules involved are locally free. The compatibility
1832	  with the unit, the associator, and the unitors is verified pointwise on the
1833	  same cover.
1834	\end{proof}

1836	\section{The varying-ring stalk--tensor commutation (d.2) and the unconditional
1837	left-whiskering}
1838	\label{sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor}
1839	
1840	This section supplies the one genuinely Mathlib-absent ingredient on which the
1841	\emph{unconditional} associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} rests: the commutation
1842	of the stalk functor with the relative-ring tensor product of presheaves of modules
1843	(``d.2''). Together with the already-built \(R_x\)-linear stalk comparison
1844	(\cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}, ``d.1'') it closes the single open left-whiskering
1845	obligation for an \emph{arbitrary} whiskering object --- with no flatness and no
1846	local-triviality hypothesis --- which is exactly what makes the sheafification
1847	transport of the presheaf associator work for all modules. The construction is to be
1848	formalized in the dedicated Lean file
1849	\(\mathtt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate/StalkTensor.lean}\).
1850	
1851	\begin{lemma}
1852	
1853	\leanok
1854	[Stalk of a tensor product (d.2): varying-ring stalk--tensor commutation]
1855	  \label{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}
1856	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.stalkTensorIso}
1857	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:stalk_linear_map}
1858	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag (Modules on Ringed Spaces, \S Tensor product),
1859	  % Lemma lemma-stalk-tensor-product (stalk of a tensor product of modules is the
1860	  % tensor product of the stalks over the stalk ring)
1861	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L2332-L2344).
1862	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1863	  % "\begin{lemma}
1864	  % \label{lemma-stalk-tensor-product}
1865	  % Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
1866	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$, $\mathcal{G}$ be $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
1867	  % Let $x \in X$. There is a canonical isomorphism
1868	  % of $\mathcal{O}_{X, x}$-modules
1869	  % $$

1877	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-stalk-tensor-product}.}
1878	  Let \(R : (\mathtt{Opens}\,X)^{op} \to \mathtt{CommRingCat}\) be the structure
1879	  presheaf of a scheme \(X\), and let \(A, B\) be presheaves of
1880	  \(R\)-modules (objects of \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,(R \circ
1881	  \mathtt{forget_2}\,\mathtt{CommRingCat}\,\mathtt{RingCat})\)). For each point
1882	  \(x \in X\) there is a canonical isomorphism of \((R \circ
1883	  \mathtt{forget_2}).\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-modules
1884	  \[
1885	    (A \otimes^{p} B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x
1886	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1887	    A.\mathtt{stalk}\,x \;\otimes_{R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x}\; B.\mathtt{stalk}\,x,
1888	  \]
1889	  natural in \(A\) and \(B\), where \(\otimes^{p}\) is the presheaf-of-modules tensor
1890	  product \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\).
1891	\end{lemma}

1893	\begin{proof}
1894	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:stalk_linear_map}
1895	  \leanok
1896	  The stalk at \(x\) is the filtered colimit over the neighbourhoods \(U \ni x\) of
1897	  the sections functor, and sectionwise the tensor product is computed pointwise:
1898	  \((A \otimes^{p} B)(U) = A(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U)\)
1899	  (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj\_obj}\)). The neighbourhood system
1900	  \(\{U \ni x\}\) is cofiltered under inclusion, so the colimit is filtered. The
1901	  isomorphism is assembled in five named stages. Stages (i)--(ii) construct the
1902	  forward additive comparison map and its germ characterisation; stages (iii)--(v)
1903	  upgrade it to an \(R_x\)-linear map, construct the reverse map, and check mutual
1904	  inversion.
1905	
1906	  \emph{(i) Per-neighbourhood balanced bilinear map and its section-level descent}
1907	  (\(\mathtt{stalkTensorBilin}\), \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDescU}\)). For a fixed
1908	  neighbourhood \(U \ni x\) the assignment
1909	  \[
1910	    (a, b) \;\longmapsto\; \mathrm{germ}_x\,a \,\otimes\, \mathrm{germ}_x\,b,
1911	    \qquad a \in A(U),\ b \in B(U),
1912	  \]
1913	  is \(R(U)\)-balanced bilinear into \(A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x\): it is additive in each
1914	  variable, and for \(r \in R(U)\) the two ways of moving \(r\) across the tensor agree
1915	  after passing to germs, since \(\mathrm{germ}_x(r \cdot a) = (\mathrm{germ}_x r)
1916	  \cdot \mathrm{germ}_x a\) and the stalk tensor is balanced over
1917	  \(R_x = R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\) (the germ--scalar compatibility of
1918	  \cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}). By the universal property of the relative tensor
1919	  product it descends to an additive map on the section module
1920	  \[
1921	    \mathtt{stalkTensorDescU}_U \;\colon\;
1922	      (A \otimes^{p} B)(U) = A(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U)
1923	        \;\longrightarrow\; A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x,
1924	    \qquad a \otimes b \longmapsto \mathrm{germ}_x a \otimes \mathrm{germ}_x b.
1925	  \]
1926	
1927	  \emph{(ii) Colimit descent to the forward comparison map}
1928	  (\(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc}\), \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc\_germ\_tmul}\)). The
1929	  maps \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDescU}_U\) are compatible with the restriction maps of the
1930	  presheaf \(A \otimes^{p} B\) as \(U\) shrinks toward \(x\) (a smaller neighbourhood
1931	  computes the same germs), so they form a cocone over the filtered diagram
1932	  \(\{(A \otimes^{p} B)(U)\}_{U \ni x}\). The induced map out of the colimit is the
1933	  forward additive comparison map
1934	  \[
1935	    \mathtt{stalkTensorDesc} \;\colon\;
1936	      (A \otimes^{p} B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x \;\longrightarrow\; A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x,
1937	  \]
1938	  characterised on germs of simple tensors by
1939	  \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc}\bigl(\mathrm{germ}_x(a \otimes b)\bigr) =
1940	  \mathrm{germ}_x a \otimes \mathrm{germ}_x b\)
1941	  (\(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc\_germ\_tmul}\)). This is the forward half of the asserted
1942	  isomorphism (\cref{lem:stalk_tensor_desc_forward}).
1943	
1944	  \emph{(iii) \(R_x\)-linearity packaging} (\(\mathtt{stalkTensorLinearMap}\)). The
1945	  additive map of (ii) is upgraded to an \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear map: on a germ
1946	  \(\mathrm{germ}_x r\) of a section \(r \in R(U)\) one checks
1947	  \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc}\bigl((\mathrm{germ}_x r)\cdot \xi\bigr) =
1948	  (\mathrm{germ}_x r)\cdot \mathtt{stalkTensorDesc}(\xi)\) on germs of simple tensors
1949	  and extends by additivity. The one subtlety is a \emph{carrier-duality obstacle}:
1950	  the section-level tensor \(A(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U)\) is a module over the
1951	  \(\mathtt{RingCat}\) carrier \((R \circ \mathtt{forget_2})(U)\), whereas the natural
1952	  scalar \(r : R(U)\) lives in the \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) carrier \(R(U)\); the two
1953	  carriers are canonically identified but not definitionally equal, so the
1954	  scalar-pulling lemma \(\mathtt{TensorProduct.smul\_tmul'}\) applies only after
1955	  inserting a canonical \(\mathtt{RingEquiv}\) bridge between the
1956	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) and \(\mathtt{RingCat}\) carriers. Once that bridge is
1957	  in place the linearity computation mirrors the germ-representative pattern used
1958	  for the d.1 stalk map \(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap}\) (\cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}).
1959	
1960	  \emph{(iv) The reverse map.} The map
1961	  \[
1962	    A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x \;\longrightarrow\; (A \otimes^{p} B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x
1963	  \]
1964	  is built by the universal property of the tensor product over \(R_x\) from the
1965	  \(R_x\)-bilinear map of the two stalks
1966	  \[
1967	    (\mathrm{germ}_x a,\ \mathrm{germ}_x b) \;\longmapsto\;
1968	      \mathrm{germ}_x\bigl(a|_{U \cap V} \otimes b|_{U \cap V}\bigr),
1969	  \]
1970	  where \(a \in A(U)\) and \(b \in B(V)\) are chosen representatives; this is a
1971	  \emph{nested} colimit descent, descending first the \(A\)-variable out of the
1972	  filtered colimit \(\{A(U)\}_{U \ni x}\) and then the \(B\)-variable out of
1973	  \(\{B(V)\}_{V \ni x}\), passing to the common refinement \(U \cap V\) to form the
1974	  simple tensor and its germ. Well-definedness on germs (independence of the chosen
1975	  representatives and of the neighbourhoods \(U, V\)) is exactly filteredness of the
1976	  neighbourhood system.
1977	
1978	  It remains to check that the reverse map is \(R_x\)-bilinear, and in particular that
1979	  its underlying bilinear map is \emph{balanced} over \(R_x\), i.e. that pulling a scalar
1980	  out of the left variable agrees with pulling it out of the right. The key point is to
1981	  establish this balancing \emph{at the stalk level}, with every scalar living in the
1982	  stalk ring \(R_x = R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\) throughout. Concretely, for a germ
1983	  \(\mathrm{germ}_x r\) of a section \(r \in R(W)\) one verifies the identity
1984	  \[
1985	    \mathtt{revBihom}\bigl((\mathrm{germ}_x r)\cdot \mathrm{germ}_x a,\ \mathrm{germ}_x b\bigr)

1987	  \]

1994	  so the action of \(r\) is transparently the \(R_x\)-action on the stalk and may be
1995	  moved freely across the tensor of stalks, where balancedness over \(R_x\) is built into
1996	  \(A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x\). Because the whole computation takes place over the single
1997	  ring \(R_x\), no carrier identification intervenes and the balancing follows directly.
1998	
1999	  One must \emph{not} instead reduce this balancing to a section-level identity. If the
2000	  scalar were pushed through the presheaf restriction to a common neighbourhood \(W\) and

2005	  \(R(W)\) that annotates the section tensor. This is precisely the
2006	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-vs-\(\mathtt{RingCat}\) carrier-duality obstacle already met in
2007	  stage (iii), here compounded by the extra restriction-of-scalars wrapper, so the naive
2008	  ``move the scalar across the tensor'' step (\(\mathtt{TensorProduct.smul\_tmul'}\)) does
2009	  not apply at the section level without first inserting the carrier bridge. Working at the
2010	  stalk level as above sidesteps this wrapper entirely.

2591	with \(\otimes\) for every morphism. The one genuine subtlety is purely structural to
2592	the formalisation: the project carries \(f^*\) \emph{abstractly}, as the left adjoint

2594	no sectionwise value to which one could literally attach the \(\mathtt{extendScalars}\)
2595	tensorator. The resolution is the standard one for an abstract left adjoint: build the
2596	strong-monoidal structure on a \emph{concrete} model \(P\) of the pullback and transport
2597	it to \(f^*\) along the uniqueness of left adjoints. This dissolves the

2602	It is worth recording why two tempting shortcuts are not taken. First, one might hope
2603	that since pullback is \emph{some} (op)lax monoidal functor, invertible objects are
2604	preserved for free. This is \emph{false}: global sections \(\Gamma\) is lax monoidal,
2605	yet \(\Gamma(\mathbb{P}^1, \mathcal{O}(1)) = 0\) is not an invertible module, so a mere
2606	oplax comparison map does \emph{not} suffice --- one genuinely has to prove the tensor
2607	comparison is an \emph{isomorphism}. Second, one might route through local triviality,
2608	``invertible \(\Rightarrow\) locally \(\mathcal{O}\) \(\Rightarrow\) pulls back to
2609	locally \(\mathcal{O}\)''. This is a mirage on the present carrier: recovering

2620	\begin{lemma}
2621	[Pullback commutes with $\otimes$ for a general scheme morphism]

2628	  of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules, the canonical comparison
2629	  \[
2630	    f^*(M \otimes_X N)

2652	  \texttt{lemma-tensor-product-pullback}: for a morphism of ringed spaces
2653	  \(f : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)\) and \(\mathcal{O}_Y\)-modules

2658	\begin{proof}
2659	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
2660	  Mathematically, pullback is extension of scalars and extension of scalars is strong
2661	  monoidal, so the comparison is an isomorphism for every \(f\). The only work is to
2662	  carry this through the formalisation's \emph{abstract} pullback \(f^* =
2663	  (\mathtt{pushforward}\,f).\mathtt{leftAdjoint}\), which has no sectionwise value. We
2664	  do not compute \(f^*\) sectionwise; instead we equip a \emph{concrete} model of the
2665	  pullback with a strong-monoidal structure and transport it to \(f^*\) along the
2666	  uniqueness of left adjoints. This mirrors Mathlib's own construction of the
2667	  extension-of-scalars monoidal structure in
2668	  \texttt{Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Monoidal/Adjunction.lean}, where the
2669	  \emph{left} adjoint's strong-monoidal structure is built explicitly (via the
2670	  base-change distributivity isomorphism) and only afterwards is the right adjoint's
2671	  lax structure derived from the adjunction --- never the reverse direction. The
2672	  argument has three steps.
2673	
2674	  \emph{Step 1 (a concrete strong-monoidal pullback \(P\)).} Build the concrete functor
2675	  \[
2676	    P \;=\; \mathtt{sheafification} \;\circ\; (\text{presheaf base change}),
2677	  \]
2678	  the presheaf-level inverse image (sectionwise extension of scalars along the
2679	  structure-sheaf ring maps of \(f\)) followed by sheafification on the small Zariski
2680	  site --- the same \(\mathtt{sheafificationCompPullback}\) shape already used in
2681	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}. Equip \(P\) with a strong-monoidal structure. Its
2682	  tensorator is the linear-algebra strong-monoidal core
2683	  \(\mathtt{TensorProduct.AlgebraTensorModule.distribBaseChange}\),
2684	  \[
2685	    (M \otimes_R N) \otimes_R S
2686	      \;\cong\;
2687	    (M \otimes_R S) \otimes_S (N \otimes_R S),
2688	  \]
2689	  an isomorphism for \emph{every} ring map \(R \to S\), with no flatness, sheafified
2690	  pointwise; this is precisely Mathlib's \((\mathtt{extendScalars}\,\varphi).\mathtt{Monoidal}\)
2691	  lifted to sheaves. The unit comparison is the already-landed sheafification-monoidality
2692	  brick \(\mathtt{sheafifyTensorUnitIso}\). Thus \(P\) is strong monoidal with each
2693	  tensorator and the unit comparison an isomorphism, with no hypothesis on \(f\).
2694	
2695	  \emph{Step 2 (\(P\) is left adjoint to pushforward).} Exhibit an adjunction
2696	  \(P \dashv \mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforward}\,f\). This is the universal property
2697	  of sheafified extension of scalars: the presheaf base change is left adjoint to
2698	  presheaf restriction of scalars, and sheafification is left adjoint to the inclusion,
2699	  so the composite \(P\) is left adjoint to the pushforward.
2700	
2701	  \emph{Step 3 (transport to the abstract pullback).} By definition
2702	  \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\,f = (\mathtt{pushforward}\,f).\mathtt{leftAdjoint}\)
2703	  is \emph{a} left adjoint of the pushforward. By the uniqueness of left adjoints
2704	  \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\) --- the same device that closed
2705	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} --- there is a canonical natural isomorphism
2706	  \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\,f \cong P\). The strong-monoidal structure of
2707	  \(P\) travels along this isomorphism, so its tensorator becomes a genuine
2708	  \emph{isomorphism}
2709	  \[
2710	    f^*(M \otimes_X N)
2711	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
2712	    (f^*M) \otimes_Y (f^*N),
2713	  \]
2714	  natural in \(M\) and \(N\). This is the asserted comparison; the abstract pullback is
2715	  never evaluated sectionwise --- the monoidal structure is transported across the
2716	  adjoint-uniqueness isomorphism.
2717	
2718	  Two shortcuts are deliberately avoided. The bundled doctrinal adjunction
2719	  \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointOplaxMonoidal}\) is present at the pinned commit, but
2720	  it is not usable here: it requires \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{SheafOfModules})\)
2721	  instances (the project carries \(\otimes_X\) as a bespoke sheafified tensor with no
2722	  such instance and no \(\mathtt{pushforward.LaxMonoidal}\)), and in any case it would
2723	  yield only an \emph{oplax} comparison \emph{map}, not the required isomorphism. And a
2724	  mere oplax map does not suffice: an (op)lax monoidal functor does \emph{not} preserve
2725	  invertible objects for free --- global sections \(\Gamma\) is lax monoidal yet
2726	  \(\Gamma(\mathbb{P}^1, \mathcal{O}(1)) = 0\) is not invertible --- so proving the
2727	  comparison is an isomorphism, as Step~3 does, is genuinely necessary.
2728	
2729	  The full strong-monoidal packaging of \(f^*\) is delivered by the transported
2730	  structure \(P.\mathtt{Monoidal}\); the invertibility consumer
2731	  \cref{lem:isinvertible_pullback} consumes only the comparison \emph{isomorphism} at
2732	  its witness pair, which Step~3 supplies.
2733	\end{proof}

2756	  but the functoriality of the structure-sheaf ring maps, so the identity holds on the
2757	  nose after the comparison of sections. This is the right-adjoint half of the unit

2767	  \((h \mathbin{;} f)\)-pushforward sections with iterated \(f_*\,h_*\)-pushforward
2768	  sections. The composition coherence is therefore the functoriality identity
2769	  \((h \mathbin{;} f)^\sharp = h^\sharp \circ f^\sharp\) of the structure-sheaf ring maps,
2770	  which holds definitionally; the two sides agree section by section.

2787	      \;=\;
2788	    (\mathtt{pullbackComp}\,h\,f).\mathrm{inv} \;\mathbin{;}\;
2789	      (\mathtt{pullback}\,h).\mathrm{map}\bigl(\mathrm{pbu}\,f\bigr) \;\mathbin{;}\;
2790	      \mathrm{pbu}\,h,
2791	  \]
2792	  where \(\mathtt{pullbackComp}\,h\,f : (h \mathbin{;} f)^* \cong h^* \circ f^*\) is the
2793	  pseudofunctoriality isomorphism of the pullback. This is the genuinely-new ingredient
2794	  for \cref{lem:pullback_unit_iso}: the left-adjoint pullback is defined abstractly, with
2795	  no sectionwise value, so this identity is \emph{not} a sectionwise statement and cannot
2796	  be checked on sections.
2797	\end{lemma}
2798	
2799	\begin{proof}
2800	  \uses{lem:unitToPushforwardObjUnit_comp}
2801	  The pullback \(f^*\) is the left adjoint of the pushforward \(f_*\), and likewise for
2802	  \(h\) and \(h \mathbin{;} f\); the comparison \(\mathrm{pbu}\,f\) is the adjunction
2803	  \emph{mate} of the pushforward comparison \(\mathrm{upu}\,f\)
2804	  (\cref{lem:unitToPushforwardObjUnit_comp}) across the pullback--pushforward adjunction,
2805	  and similarly for \(h\) and \(h \mathbin{;} f\). Since the mate correspondence is a
2806	  bijection, the asserted identity follows once both sides are transposed under the
2807	  adjunction \((h \mathbin{;} f)^* \dashv (h \mathbin{;} f)_*\).
2808	
2809	  Under this transpose the left-hand side \(\mathrm{pbu}(h \mathbin{;} f)\) becomes the
2810	  pushforward comparison \(\mathrm{upu}(h \mathbin{;} f)\). The right-hand side, a
2811	  composite involving \((\mathtt{pullbackComp}\,h\,f).\mathrm{inv}\) and the mates of
2812	  \(\mathrm{pbu}\,f\) and \(\mathrm{pbu}\,h\), transposes --- using the conjugation of the
2813	  pseudofunctoriality isomorphisms (\(\mathtt{pullbackComp}\,h\,f\) on the left adjoints
2814	  corresponds to \(\mathtt{pushforwardComp}\,h\,f\) on the right adjoints) and the unit of
2815	  the composite adjunction \((f^* \dashv f_*) \circ (h^* \dashv h_*)\) --- exactly into the
2816	  right-hand side of the pushforward coherence
2817	  \cref{lem:unitToPushforwardObjUnit_comp}, namely
2818	  \(\mathrm{upu}\,f \mathbin{;} f_*(\mathrm{upu}\,h) \mathbin{;}
2819	  (\mathtt{pushforwardComp}\,h\,f).\mathrm{hom}\). Thus the two transposed sides coincide
2820	  by \cref{lem:unitToPushforwardObjUnit_comp}, and the original identity follows by the
2821	  injectivity of the transpose.
2822	\end{proof}

2824	\begin{lemma}
2825	
2826	\leanok
2827	[Pullback preserves the structure sheaf]
2828	  \label{lem:pullback_unit_iso}
2829	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackUnitIso}
2830	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
2831	  Let \(f : Y \to X\) be a morphism of schemes. The pullback of the structure sheaf
2832	  is the structure sheaf: there is a canonical isomorphism
2833	  \[
2834	    f^*\bigl(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\bigr)
2835	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
2836	    \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,Y.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf},
2837	    \qquad\text{i.e.}\qquad
2838	    f^* \mathcal{O}_X \;\cong\; \mathcal{O}_Y,
2839	  \]
2840	  where \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf} = \mathcal{O}_X\) is
2841	  the monoidal unit of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\).
2842	\end{lemma}
2843	
2844	\begin{proof}
2845	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
2846	  This is a one-step consequence of a Mathlib instance, with no chart-chase. The
2847	  canonical comparison map is the genuine Mathlib morphism
2848	  \[
2849	    \mathtt{SheafOfModules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit}\,f :
2850	      f^*\mathcal{O}_X \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal{O}_Y,
2851	  \]
2852	  the unit comparison \(\eta\) of \(f^*\); abbreviate it \(\mathrm{pbu}\,f\). Mathlib
2853	  proves this map is an isomorphism whenever the comparison functor
2854	  \(\mathtt{Opens.map}\,f.\mathtt{base}\) on opens is \(\mathtt{Final}\): the instance
2855	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.instIsIsoPullbackObjUnitToUnitOfFinal}\).
2856	
2857	  The point is that the finality hypothesis holds \emph{unconditionally}, for every
2858	  morphism of schemes \(f\). The preimage functor \(\mathtt{Opens.map}\,f.\mathtt{base}
2859	  : \mathtt{Opens}\,X \to \mathtt{Opens}\,Y\) is a frame homomorphism: it preserves
2860	  arbitrary unions and finite intersections, hence preserves all finite limits. A
2861	  functor between small categories that preserves finite limits is representably flat,
2862	  so \(\mathtt{final\_of\_representablyFlat}\) gives \((\mathtt{Opens.map}\,f.\mathtt{base}).\mathtt{Final}\)
2863	  with no hypothesis on \(f\) whatsoever. (There is no need to pass to affine charts:
2864	  finality is not a special feature of open immersions but a general property of
2865	  preimage-on-opens.)
2866	
2867	  Consequently the instance \(\mathtt{instIsIsoPullbackObjUnitToUnitOfFinal}\) fires
2868	  directly, and \(\mathrm{pbu}\,f\) is an isomorphism for every \(f\). The asserted
2869	  isomorphism \(f^*\mathcal{O}_X \cong \mathcal{O}_Y\) is the bundled iso
2870	  \(\mathtt{pullbackUnitIso}\,f\) of this comparison.
2871	\end{proof}
2872	
2873	\begin{lemma}
2874	[Pullback preserves $\otimes$-invertibility for a general scheme morphism]
2875	  \label{lem:isinvertible_pullback}
2876	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.IsInvertible.pullback}
2877	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible, lem:pullback_tensor_iso,
2878	        lem:pullback_unit_iso}
2879	  Let \(f : Y \to X\) be a morphism of schemes and let
2880	  \(M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). If \(M\) is \(\otimes\)-invertible
2881	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), then so is its pullback \(f^*M\):
2882	  \[
2883	    \mathtt{IsInvertible}(M)
2884	      \;\Longrightarrow\;
2885	    \mathtt{IsInvertible}\bigl((\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\,f).\mathrm{obj}\,M\bigr).
2886	  \]
2887	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemma
2888	  % \ref{lemma-pullback-invertible} (the pullback of an invertible module is
2889	  % invertible) (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4142-4147).
2890	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2891	  % "\begin{lemma}
2892	  % \label{lemma-pullback-invertible}
2893	  % Let $f : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$ be a
2894	  % morphism of ringed spaces. The pullback $f^*\mathcal{L}$ of an
2895	  % invertible $\mathcal{O}_Y$-module is invertible.
2896	  % \end{lemma}"
2897	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemma
2898	  \texttt{lemma-pullback-invertible}: the pullback \(f^*\mathcal{L}\) of an
2899	  invertible \(\mathcal{O}\)-module along a morphism of ringed spaces is
2900	  invertible.}
2901	\end{lemma}
2902	
2903	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4149-4157).
2904	% "\begin{proof}
2905	% By Lemma \ref{lemma-invertible}
2906	% there exists an $\mathcal{O}_Y$-module $\mathcal{N}$ such that
2907	% $\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_Y} \mathcal{N} \cong \mathcal{O}_Y$.
2908	% Pulling back we get
2909	% $f^*\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} f^*\mathcal{N} \cong \mathcal{O}_X$
2910	% by Lemma \ref{lemma-tensor-product-pullback}.
2911	% Thus $f^*\mathcal{L}$ is invertible by Lemma \ref{lemma-invertible}.
2912	% \end{proof}"
2913	\begin{proof}
2914	  \uses{lem:pullback_tensor_iso, lem:pullback_unit_iso}
2915	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Lemma \texttt{lemma-pullback-invertible}.}
2916	  This is the Stacks proof in the project's notation. By
2917	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(M)\) (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}) there is a
2918	  witness \(N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) together with an isomorphism
2919	  \(e : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_X\). Take the pullback witness
2920	  \((\mathtt{pullback}\,f).\mathrm{obj}\,N = f^*N\). The required isomorphism
2921	  exhibiting \(f^*M\) as \(\otimes\)-invertible is the composite
2922	  \[
2923	    (f^*M) \otimes_Y (f^*N)
2924	      \;\xrightarrow[\sim]{\;\mathtt{pullbackTensorIso}^{-1}\;}\;
2925	    f^*(M \otimes_X N)
2926	      \;\xrightarrow[\sim]{\;f^*e\;}\;
2927	    f^*\mathcal{O}_X
2928	      \;\xrightarrow[\sim]{\;\mathtt{pullbackUnitIso}\;}\;
2929	    \mathcal{O}_Y,
2930	  \]
2931	  This composite uses \cref{lem:pullback_tensor_iso} to commute the pullback past the
2932	  tensor and \cref{lem:pullback_unit_iso} to identify \(f^*\mathcal{O}_X\) with
2933	  \(\mathcal{O}_Y\). Hence \(f^*N\) is a tensor inverse of \(f^*M\), so
2934	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(f^*M)\) holds.
2935	\end{proof}

2937	\section{The invertibility-carrier Picard group}
2938	\label{sec:tensorobj_pic_carrier}
2939	
2940	The group law of \cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup} is carried on the
2941	\emph{locally-trivial} predicate \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\), and on that carrier
2942	the existence of a tensor inverse for each class is an extra obligation
2943	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}): one must \emph{manufacture} the dual
2944	object \(L^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)\) and prove the
2945	contraction \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \cong \mathcal{O}_X\), which is the whole content of
2946	the dual-infrastructure build of \cref{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra}.
2947	
2948	This section carries the group law on a \emph{different} predicate for which that
2949	obligation evaporates: the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
2950	\(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible},
2951	\[
2952	  \mathtt{IsInvertible}(M)
2953	  \;:\equiv\;
2954	  \exists\, N,\ \mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl(M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\bigr).
2955	\]
2956	On this carrier the inverse of a class \([M]\) is supplied by the \emph{membership
2957	witness itself} --- the very \(N\) whose existence \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(M)\)
2958	asserts --- so no inverse object need be constructed and no separate
2959	tensor-inverse lemma is consumed. The cost is shifted to a routine
2960	well-definedness fact (\cref{lem:isinvertible_inverse_welldef}: the witness \(N\) is
2961	determined up to isomorphism), proved from the monoidal coherence isomorphisms
2962	alone. The carrier is the scheme-theoretic analogue of Mathlib's
2963	\(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\), whose elements are exactly the iso-classes of invertible
2964	modules (Stacks tag 01CX).
2965	
2966	\begin{lemma}
2967	
2968	\leanok
2969	[Associator for $\otimes_X$ on invertible objects (corollary of the unconditional
2970	associator)]
2971	  \label{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso_invertible}
2972	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_assoc_iso_invertible}
2973	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
2974	        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}
2975	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
2976	  \(M, N, P \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be \(\otimes\)-invertible
2977	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}). There is an isomorphism
2978	  \[
2979	    (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
2980	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
2981	    M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)
2982	  \]
2983	  in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The invertibility-carrier group law
2984	  (\cref{thm:pic_commgroup}) consumes only the \emph{existence} of this
2985	  isomorphism, the proposition \(\mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
2986	  \cong M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\bigr)\); no pentagon, triangle, or naturality
2987	  coherence is ever required.
2988	\end{lemma}
2989	
2990	\begin{proof}
2991	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}
2992	  Immediate specialisation of the \emph{unconditional} associator
2993	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} to the invertible arguments \(M, N, P\); no
2994	  hypothesis on the arguments is used. (The earlier route argued via ``invertible
2995	  \(\Rightarrow\) locally free \(\Rightarrow\) sectionwise flat'' to feed the
2996	  flat-whiskering lemma \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}; that step is \emph{false}
2997	  --- local freeness does not give global sectionwise flatness \(M(U)\) over
2998	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\) for a non-affine open \(U\) --- and is no longer needed, since
2999	  the general associator is itself unconditional, its single open left-whiskering
3000	  obligation \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} being closed via the d.2
3001	  stalk--tensor commutation \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}.)
3002	\end{proof}

3004	\begin{definition}
3005	
3006	\leanok
3007	[The invertibility-carrier Picard group \(\Pic X\)]
3008	  \label{def:pic_carrier}
3009	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.PicGroup}
3010	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}
3011	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CX (Modules on Ringed Spaces, \S Invertible
3012	  % modules), Definition definition-pic (the Picard group as the abelian group of
3013	  % iso-classes of invertible modules under tensor product)
3014	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4350-L4357).
3015	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
3016	  % "\begin{definition}
3017	  % \label{definition-pic}
3018	  % Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
3019	  % The {\it Picard group} $\Pic(X)$ of $X$ is the
3020	  % abelian group whose elements are isomorphism classes of
3021	  % invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules, with addition
3022	  % corresponding to tensor product.
3023	  % \end{definition}"
3024	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CX, Definition~\texttt{definition-pic}:
3025	  the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) is the abelian group of isomorphism classes of
3026	  invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, with addition the tensor product.}
3027	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. Consider the subtype of \(\otimes\)-invertible objects
3028	  \[
3029	    \mathtt{Invertible}\,X
3030	    \;:=\;
3031	    \bigl\{\, M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
3032	              \,\bigm|\, \mathtt{IsInvertible}\,M \,\bigr\}
3033	  \]
3034	  of \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}, and equip it with the setoid whose
3035	  relation is ``there exists an isomorphism'',
3036	  \(M \sim M' :\equiv \mathrm{Nonempty}(M \cong M')\) (a proposition-valued
3037	  equivalence relation: reflexive, symmetric and transitive because isomorphisms
3038	  compose and invert). The \emph{Picard group carrier}
3039	  \[
3040	    \Pic X \;:=\; \mathtt{Invertible}\,X \,/\!\sim
3041	  \]
3042	  is the quotient type of isomorphism classes of \(\otimes\)-invertible
3043	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. We write \([M] \in \Pic X\) for the class of an
3044	  invertible \(M\). This is the scheme-theoretic analogue of Mathlib's
3045	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\), carried on \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) rather than on
3046	  \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\); on a scheme the two predicates agree (Stacks tag
3047	  0B8M), but only the invertibility form carries the inverse witness existentially.
3048	\end{definition}

3079	  invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules is again invertible.}
3080	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(M, M' \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). If

3088	  Let \(N\) be a tensor inverse of \(M\) (so \(M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\))
3089	  and \(N'\) a tensor inverse of \(M'\) (so \(M' \otimes_X N' \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)),
3090	  furnished by the two invertibility witnesses. We claim \(N \otimes_X N'\) is a
3091	  tensor inverse of \(M \otimes_X M'\). Indeed, reassociating and commuting the four
3092	  factors with the associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso_invertible} and the
3093	  braiding \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso},
3094	  \[
3095	    (M \otimes_X M') \otimes_X (N \otimes_X N')
3096	    \;\cong\;
3097	    (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X (M' \otimes_X N')
3098	    \;\cong\;
3099	    \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X
3100	    \;\cong\;
3101	    \mathcal{O}_X,
3102	  \]

3104	  unitor \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}. Hence \(N \otimes_X N'\) witnesses
3105	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(M \otimes_X M')\). Every step is consumed only as the
3106	  existence of an isomorphism, so no coherence identity is required.

3164	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
3165	  % "\begin{lemma}
3166	  % \label{lemma-invertible}

3179	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CR, Lemma~\texttt{lemma-invertible}: when
3180	  \(\mathcal{L}\) is invertible the module \(\mathcal{N}\) with
3181	  \(\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{N} \cong \mathcal{O}_X\) is
3182	  isomorphic to the dual \(\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X)\),
3183	  hence unique up to isomorphism.}
3184	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(M, N, N' \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). If
3185	  \(M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\) and \(M \otimes_X N' \cong \mathcal{O}_X\),
3186	  then \(N \cong N'\). Consequently the assignment \([M] \mapsto [N]\), sending an
3187	  invertible class to the class of its tensor inverse, is well-defined on
3188	  \(\Pic X\).

3191	\begin{proof}
3192	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,
3193	        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso_invertible, lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}
3194	  This is the standard ``inverse-of-inverse'' argument in a commutative monoid, run
3195	  with isomorphisms in place of equalities. Chain the unitor
3196	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, the two hypotheses, the associator
3197	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso_invertible} and the braiding
3198	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}:
3199	  \[
3200	    N
3201	    \;\cong\; N \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X
3202	    \;\cong\; N \otimes_X (M \otimes_X N')
3203	    \;\cong\; (N \otimes_X M) \otimes_X N'
3204	    \;\cong\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X N'
3205	    \;\cong\; N',
3206	  \]
3207	  where the second step substitutes \(\mathcal{O}_X \cong M \otimes_X N'\), the
3208	  third is the associator, the fourth uses the braiding
3209	  \(N \otimes_X M \cong M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\), and the outer two are
3210	  the unitors. Hence \(N \cong N'\). For well-definedness on classes, note that if
3211	  \(M \cong M'\) then a tensor inverse of \(M\) is also a tensor inverse of \(M'\)
3212	  (tensor with the isomorphism), so the inverse class depends only on \([M]\).
3213	\end{proof}

3253	  and Lemma~\texttt{lemma-constructions-invertible}: the isomorphism classes of
3254	  invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules form an abelian group under tensor product,
3255	  with unit \(\mathcal{O}_X\).}
3256	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The carrier \(\Pic X\) of \cref{def:pic_carrier} is an
3257	  abelian group, with
3258	  \[
3259	    [M] \cdot [M'] := [M \otimes_X M'],
3260	    \qquad
3261	    1 := [\mathcal{O}_X],
3262	    \qquad
3263	    [M]^{-1} := [N] \ \text{for any witness } M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X.
3264	  \]

3266	
3267	\begin{proof}
3268	  \uses{def:pic_carrier, lem:isinvertible_tensor, lem:isinvertible_unit,
3269	        lem:isinvertible_inverse_welldef, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,
3270	        lem:tensorobj_comm_iso, lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso_invertible}
3271	  Each group-operation datum and each axiom is supplied by a single
3272	  existence-of-isomorphism, read as an equality of classes in \(\Pic X\).
3273	
3274	  \emph{The operations are well-defined.} The product \([M] \cdot [M'] := [M
3275	  \otimes_X M']\) lands in \(\Pic X\) because \(\otimes_X\) preserves invertibility
3276	  (\cref{lem:isinvertible_tensor}), and it is independent of representatives by the
3277	  bifunctoriality of \(\otimes_X\)
3278	  (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}): an isomorphism in either
3279	  argument tensors to an isomorphism, hence an equality of classes. The unit
3280	  \([\mathcal{O}_X]\) lies in \(\Pic X\) by \cref{lem:isinvertible_unit}. The
3281	  inverse \([M] \mapsto [N]\), \(N\) any membership witness of
3282	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\,M\), is well-defined by
3283	  \cref{lem:isinvertible_inverse_welldef} --- the witness is determined up to
3284	  isomorphism, so its class depends only on \([M]\). Crucially, \emph{no inverse
3285	  object is constructed}: the inverse is read off the existential carried by
3286	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\).
3287	
3288	  \emph{The axioms.} Each axiom is the image, under passage to iso-classes, of one
3289	  coherence isomorphism of \(\otimes_X\):
3290	  \[
3291	    \mathtt{one\_mul} \leftarrow \langle \lambda \rangle,
3292	    \quad
3293	    \mathtt{mul\_one} \leftarrow \langle \rho \rangle,
3294	    \quad
3295	    \mathtt{mul\_assoc} \leftarrow \langle \alpha \rangle,
3296	    \quad
3297	    \mathtt{mul\_comm} \leftarrow \langle \beta \rangle,
3298	    \quad
3299	    \mathtt{mul\_left\_inv} \leftarrow \langle \varepsilon \rangle,
3300	  \]
3301	  where the unit laws are the unitors \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, associativity
3302	  is the invertible-case associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso_invertible},
3303	  commutativity is the braiding \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, and the left-inverse
3304	  law \([N] \cdot [M] = [N \otimes_X M] = [\mathcal{O}_X] = 1\) is the witness
3305	  isomorphism \(\varepsilon : N \otimes_X M \cong \mathcal{O}_X\) itself (obtained
3306	  from \(M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\) by the braiding). Each axiom asserts
3307	  only \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\), so no pentagon, triangle, or
3308	  hexagon coherence is invoked; commutativity makes the left-inverse law suffice
3309	  for a two-sided inverse.
3310	
3311	  This is the invertibility-carrier analogue of
3312	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}, differing in exactly one respect: the
3313	  left-inverse field is discharged by the predicate's own membership witness rather
3314	  than by the manufactured dual of \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}. The
3315	  whole dual-infrastructure obligation of \cref{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra} is
3316	  thereby off this group's critical path.
3317	\end{proof}

3319	\section{Consumer: the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance for the relative Picard sheaf}
3320	\label{sec:tensorobj_consumer_acg}
3321	
3322	The substrate of \cref{sec:tensorobj_substrate,sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
3323	supplies all the data missing from \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}'s Lean
3324	encoding. The consumer instance below packages the abelian-group law
3325	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
3326	 := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\) on the
3327	relative Picard quotient via the substrate.
3328	
3329	\begin{theorem}
3330	
3331	
3332	\leanok
3333	[Abelian-group instance on the relative Picard quotient via \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)]
3334	  \label{thm:rel_pic_addcommgroup_via_tensorobj}

3340	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
3341	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
3342	        def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
3343	        lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup,
3344	        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,
3345	        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,

3350	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
3351	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
3352	  % abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian

3356	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}

3358	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
3359	  % abelian groups defined by the formula

3366	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
3367	  % topologies  by

3371	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
3372	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the target category is the category of abelian
3373	  groups; the relative Picard functor is a quotient of abelian groups).}
3374	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a
3375	  field \(k\), let \(\pi_C : C \to \Spec k\) be the structure morphism,
3376	  and let \(T\) be a \(k\)-scheme with structure morphism
3377	  \(\pi_T : T \to \Spec k\). The set
3378	  \[
3379	    \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \,/\, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
3380	  \]
3381	  of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries a canonical
3382	  abelian-group structure with addition
3383	  \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
3384	   := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_k T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
3385	  neutral element \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\,\bigr]\), and
3386	  inverse
3387	  \(-\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\),
3388	  where \(\otimes_{C \times_k T}\) and \((-)^{-1}\) are the operations
3389	  supplied by the substrate of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
3390	  restricted to the \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carrier
3391	  via \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}. With this structure the quotient
3392	  map
3393	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is a
3394	  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly \(\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\),
3395	  in agreement with \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.

3398	\begin{proof}
3399	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
3400	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
3401	        def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
3402	        lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup,
3403	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
3404	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
3405	  Write \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) for the set of isomorphism classes of objects
3406	  of \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) (line bundles on
3407	  \(C \times_k T\)), with the operation
3408	  \([L] + [L'] := [L \otimes_{C \times_k T} L']\). This operation is
3409	  well-defined on isomorphism classes: an isomorphism \(L \cong L_0\) tensors
3410	  to an isomorphism \(L \otimes L' \cong L_0 \otimes L'\) by functoriality of
3411	  \(\otimes\) (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}), and
3412	  symmetrically in the second argument. The abelian-group structure on
3413	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is then exactly the \emph{units group of the
3414	  commutative monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes}
3415	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}): multiplication
3416	  \([L]\cdot[L'] = [L \otimes_{C\times_k T} L']\) is well-defined and
3417	  commutative-monoidal by the coherence isomorphisms
3418	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso},
3419	  and an iso-class is a unit precisely when its carrier is
3420	  \(\otimes\)-invertible (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), the inverse
3421	  being carried by the predicate. Each axiom is a \emph{proposition} --- the
3422	  existence of an isomorphism --- so no monoidal-coherence datum (pentagon,
3423	  triangle, hexagon) is invoked; the \(\AddCommGroup\) on
3424	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the additive form of this units group. This mirrors
3425	  Mathlib's own Picard group: \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\) carries its
3426	  \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) (\(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\),
3427	  \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) transported from
3428	  \(\mathrm{Units}\bigl(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R)\bigr)\); here
3429	  the same \(\mathrm{Units}(\cdots)\) shape is transported to the scheme-level
3430	  \(\otimes_X\). (The scheme-level Picard group is genuinely absent from

### MAJOR FINDING 1: `presheafPushforwardLaxMonoidal` and `presheafPullbackOplaxMonoidal` are unpin­ned in the blueprint

**Severity**: **major**

Two substantive infrastructure instances were added this iteration to `TensorObjSubstrate.lean`:

1. `presheafPushforwardLaxMonoidal` (L1137–1147): noncomputable instance establishing that `PresheafOfModules.pushforward φ` is lax monoidal for a CommRingCat-level presheaf ring morphism φ. Full proof body; no sorry.

2. `presheafPullbackOplaxMonoidal` (L1159–1166): noncomputable instance establishing that `PresheafOfModules.pullback φ` is oplax monoidal with canonical comparison δ, built via `(pullbackPushforwardAdjunction φ).leftAdjointOplaxMonoidal`. Full proof body; no sorry.

Neither is currently pinned by any `\lean{...}` block in the blueprint. These declarations are reusable prerequisites for `pullbackTensorIso` (and are described narratively in the Phase-2 status comment at L1168–1194 of the Lean file).

**Recommended chapter-side action**: Add a new blueprint block (or extend the existing `lem:presheaf_pushforward_adj_substrate` block) pinning these two instances. Suggested label: `lem:presheaf_pullback_oplax_monoidal`. The block should note these are at the presheaf level (not the SheafOfModules level) and serve as the reusable Phase-2 prerequisites toward `pullbackTensorIso`.

### MAJOR FINDING 2: Blueprint proof sketch for `lem:pullback_tensor_iso` is stale regarding `pushforward.LaxMonoidal`

**Severity**: **major**

The blueprint proof sketch for `lem:pullback_tensor_iso` (lines ~2718–2728) states:

> "The bundled doctrinal adjunction `Adjunction.leftAdjointOplaxMonoidal` is present at the pinned commit, but it is **not usable here**: it requires `MonoidalCategory(SheafOfModules)` instances (the project carries `⊗_X` as a bespoke sheafified tensor with no such instance and **no `pushforward.LaxMonoidal`**), and in any case it would yield only an oplax comparison map, not the required isomorphism."

The claim "**no `pushforward.LaxMonoidal`**" is now **refuted** by iter-242 work:
- `presheafPushforwardLaxMonoidal` builds `(PresheafOfModules.pushforward φ).LaxMonoidal` at the presheaf level.
- `presheafPullbackOplaxMonoidal` uses `leftAdjointOplaxMonoidal` to build the oplax structure — precisely the "not usable here" shortcut the blueprint says is unavailable.

The conclusion remains correct (the oplax map alone doesn't suffice to prove `δ` is an iso, as noted in the Lean file's Phase-2 comment: no "oplax ⇒ preserves invertibles" lemma), but the stated reason is wrong. The real remaining obstacle is the **Mathlib-absent concrete inverse-image model** (`PresheafOfModules.extendScalars` + left-Kan-extension topological inverse image).

**Recommended chapter-side action**: Update the proof sketch for `lem:pullback_tensor_iso` to:
1. Acknowledge that `presheafPushforwardLaxMonoidal` IS now built (presheaf-level `pushforward.LaxMonoidal`).
2. Acknowledge that `presheafPullbackOplaxMonoidal` IS now built (abstract pullback is oplax monoidal with comparison `δ`).
3. Clarify that the remaining obstacle is: δ is not automatically an iso (Γ(ℙ¹, 𝒪(1))=0 counterexample), and proving iso-ness requires a concrete model of the pullback — specifically `PresheafOfModules.extendScalars` + left-Kan-extension inverse image, which are Mathlib-absent at the pinned commit.
